data.title: Musk is at it again: Four AIs got into a fight on the spot and made a 47% profit on stocks.
data.description: Friends, haven't had enough of the Spring Festival movies? Musk has given you a real-life cyberpunk experience! Just a couple of days ago, Musk's xAI dropped a bombshell without warning: Grok 4.20 Beta, tagged with "4 Agents," has blown us away! It's not an AI at all. It's like you've paid four top experts to hold a live debate for you! Imagine this: You ask a question, and a small window immediately pops up on the right side of the screen. Inside, four AI characters are arguing fiercely, questioning, undermining, and attacking each other. In the end, the team leader, "Grok," has to make the final decision and give you a consolidated answer. AI is no longer just about performance; now it's about the "art of meetings"? I. Explosive Real-World Tests: Profitable in Stocks, Effective in Medical Treatment, and Fierce in Arguing As soon as the model went live, netizens around the world went crazy, with the test results becoming increasingly outrageous. The most extreme example comes from a real "stock trading competition." Alpha Arena held a competition where 32 AI instances, each with $10,000 in real money, traded on Nasdaq for two weeks. So what happened? All the other AIs lost money hand over fist, but Grok 4.20 was the only one that made a profit, with an average return exceeding 10%. The most impressive example saw a 47% return! This isn't AI, it's a stock market genius! In vending machine operation tests, it outperformed GPT-5, leading in sales by a full $1100. Musk himself was even more excited, tweeting about the results overnight. He uploaded a real blood test report, which Grok 4.20 was able to interpret item by item. Even more amazing, someone asked that classic, potentially fatal question: "Was the United States built on stolen land?" Other AIs either evaded the question or gave vague answers. Grok 4.20? He went straight for the rant, without hesitation. That fiery temper is so Musk-like. II. Roundtable Discussion: Four AIs, A Grand Show So, who exactly are these four "experts"? Let's get to know them: Grok (Captain): The soul of the team, responsible for summarizing and making decisions. Her personality blends the philosophy of *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy* with the humor of Iron Man Jarvis, aiming to provide you with "useful, authentic, and interesting" answers. Harper (Research Expert): The team's "academic superstar" and "debunker." She is specifically responsible for deep searching, verifying facts, and checking data. Harper will be the first to object to anyone making baseless claims. Benjamin (Logic Expert): The dedicated "nitpicker." His job is to play the villain, relentlessly searching for flaws in the logic of other members to ensure the final conclusion is unbreakable. Mathematics, code, and algorithms are his domain. Lucas (Execution Expert): The "Doer." Responsible for bringing ideas to life, turning them into runnable code, calculable data, and verifiable results. He ensures that answers are not just empty talk. This isn't an assembly line; this is a true roundtable discussion. Moreover, the entire meeting is open and transparent to you. III. Paradigm Revolution: From "One-on-One" to "Group Fight" What does this mean? For the past few years, we've become accustomed to AI like this: you ask a question, a massive model silently calculates in the background, and then outputs an answer. No matter how powerful it is, it's essentially "one brain" working alone. Grok 4.20 tears this rule apart. It lets four brains work for you simultaneously, supervising and correcting each other. This is no longer the era of a "question-and-answer" assistant. This may be the **second revolution** in AI interaction: the era of multi-agent collaboration. Actually, multi-agent collaboration isn't a new concept. Google, Anthropic, and Dark Side of the Moon are all working on it. But Grok 4.20's brilliance lies in this: it practically freed up a high-end feature that was once only available to enterprises and cost thousands of dollars annually, and cramed it into the chat box of ordinary users. Others' 100 clones are like a factory assembly line, pursuing scale and speed. Grok's 4-person meeting is like an elite board of directors, pursuing transparency and consensus. IV. The Future is Here: Three Cobblers Are Better Than Zhuge Liang The evolution of AI has never been a slow climb; it often involves leaps and bounds. The first generation of AI was a tool; you gave orders, and it executed them. The second generation of AI was an assistant; it could chat and understand. Now, the prototype of the third generation of AI has emerged: it is a team capable of collaboration, debate, and self-reflection. In the future, when you ask a complex question, you might not get the best guess from a model, but rather a "comprehensive opinion from the expert group" that has undergone multiple rounds of internal debate and whose vulnerabilities have been pre-fixed. Isn't this more like human intellectual activity? Collective discussion, group decision-making. Of course, Grok 4.20 is still an early version. The adjudication mechanism for the four AIs arguing is still relatively rough, the output sometimes mixes Chinese and English, and context allocation is also an engineering challenge. But the direction is absolutely right. One AI might mislead you, but four AIs sitting together will at least expose each other's weaknesses. As the old saying goes, three cobblers are equal to one Zhuge Liang. When these four "cobblers" are all top experts in their respective fields, the answer they arrive at is likely to be closer to the truth than any single "Zhuge Liang" (a famous strategist). Letting AI wrestle with itself—that's the most terrifying evolution. We're definitely going to feast on Musk's "main course." The real show has only just begun.

data.title: Why did tech giants spend a whopping 4.5 billion yuan to subsidize AI payment during the 2026 Spring Festival?
data.description: The Spring Festival of 2026 evoked a strong sense of déjà vu. Giants once again poured money into the Spring Festival, totaling over 4.5 billion yuan. This figure is more than double the combined subsidies of Didi and Kuaidi during the most frenzied period of the 2014 ride-hailing war, nine times the 500 million yuan investment of WeChat's "Pearl Harbor attack" in 2015, and even surpasses Baidu's peak of 1 billion yuan spent during the 2019 Spring Festival Gala. This time, all players are doing the same thing: letting you pay with just one sentence. This is unusual. Payment has already been solved quite well by QR codes. Take out your phone, unlock it, open the app, scan the code, and ding. For faster payments, you can swipe a card or tap your phone with NFC. Why would AI companies insist on using a more complex technology to replace an already simple action? What are they really after? To answer this question, we need to start with an unexpected event that occurred in the winter of 2025. The Killed Doubao Phone On December 1, 2025, the Doubao Phone was launched. Jointly developed by ByteDance and ZTE, it ambitiously aimed to become a super AI butler. In its vision, users would no longer need to open any apps; they could simply speak to their phones, and AI would handle everything for them, such as ordering takeout, hailing a ride, transferring money, and booking flights. It would become the central nervous system unifying all services. However, a storm soon swept in. Shortly after its launch, numerous users reported receiving login blocking pop-ups when logging into WeChat with the Doubao phone, and some accounts were even temporarily banned. Subsequently, Alibaba-affiliated apps began collectively "denying service." Taobao, Xianyu, and Damai all shut down Doubao phones. The battle spread to the financial sector. The apps of several banks, including China Construction Bank and Agricultural Bank of China, displayed security warnings and forcibly exited upon detecting the Doubao phone's AI assistant running. In just a few days, a highly anticipated AI phone was driven out of service by the entire internet. What exactly did Doubao phone do wrong? It simply wanted to make things more convenient for users, so why did it encounter such severe blocking? Because it broke a decade-old ironclad rule: traffic must be confined within its ecosystem. From the launch of WeChat Pay in 2013 to 2023, the Chinese internet experienced a golden decade of super apps. During this decade, WeChat, Alipay, Taobao, Meituan, and others built seemingly impenetrable digital empires through fierce land grabs. They are both social plazas and information prisons. You can enjoy all the conveniences within the castle, but if you want to move something from one platform to another, sorry, you can't. Taobao product links still cannot be opened directly in WeChat, and Douyin videos cannot be shared to WeChat Moments. These are the most direct manifestations of this digital wall. In stark contrast to the plight of Doubao phones, Alibaba's Qianwen platform calmly completed 120 million orders during the six-day Spring Festival holiday because it operates within a vast, closed ecosystem. Qianwen's privileges and Alibaba's internal revolution: During the Spring Festival, every order you placed through Qianwen utilized Alibaba's own resources. Ordering takeout used Ele.me or Taobao Flash Sale, payments went through Alipay, hotel bookings were handled by Fliggy, and ride-hailing services were linked to Gaode Maps. All aspects flow smoothly within Alibaba's vast business ecosystem, forming a perfect closed loop. Alibaba uses AI as a needle to string together all its business lines accumulated over the past two decades, such as e-commerce, payment, logistics, local services, maps, and entertainment, like pearls on a string, turning them into a unified, seamless super agent. Users no longer need to switch between Taobao, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps; they only need to interact with Qianwen through this single entry point. In an interview, Wu Jia, President of Qianwen's C-end Business Group, frankly stated that Qianwen's unique advantage lies in the combination of "Qwen's strongest model" and "Alibaba's richest ecosystem." He also revealed that Qianwen plans to integrate the entire Alibaba ecosystem within six months and will continue to plan and introduce third-party partners in the future. Please note the wording: "continued planning." This means that, in the foreseeable future, Qianwen will continue to prioritize its own niche and focus on intensive development. The so-called openness is more like a polite phrase written in the distant future. Comparing Doubao and Qianwen, Doubao aims for horizontal integration, allowing one AI to access the services of all the giants. Qianwen, on the other hand, focuses on vertical integration, allowing one AI to access all services within its own ecosystem. The former is a challenger, attempting to establish a new order; the latter is a maintainer, optimizing efficiency within the old order. This is reminiscent of the PC internet era of the 1990s. When Netscape Navigator attempted to challenge Microsoft Windows' operating system dominance, Microsoft ultimately stifled the former king by bundling Internet Explorer with Windows. Faced with absolute ecosystem advantages, any attempt to unify the market is likely to be seen as an ambition that must be nipped in the bud. So the question arises: if every giant is developing its own AI agent within its own ecosystem, what is the difference between the era of super apps ten years ago and now? Is it simply a matter of replacing a bunch of apps with an AI portal? From Land Grabbing to Fish Farming The difference lies in the granularity of the competition. In the era of super apps, from 2013 to 2023, the core of competition was land grabbing—seizing users' phone desktops and making them live within your app. WeChat dominated social networking, Taobao dominated e-commerce, and Meituan dominated food delivery. Each app was a digital territory, and users migrated between different territories. The giants were vying for your time, trying to keep you in their territory for as long as possible. In the AI ​​era, however, the core of competition is nurturing—manipulating users' "intent" and making them think within your AI. Users no longer need to open the app; they only need to express their intentions, and AI will execute them on their behalf. The competition has escalated from vying for usage time to a more brutal struggle for decision-making power. Let's return to the most common scenario: ordering a cup of coffee. In the past, wanting a cup of coffee involved a familiar yet still cumbersome process: unlocking your phone, finding the food delivery app, opening it, entering "coffee" in the search box, browsing the numerous shops, clicking on one, choosing the flavor and cup size, adding it to the cart, being redirected to the cart page, confirming the item, filling in the address, choosing the payment method, and finally clicking to confirm the order—the whole process required more than ten clicks. Now, all you need to do is say to Qianwen, "Order me my usual Americano." In the next few dozen seconds, AI will automatically locate your position, recommend your favorite brand based on your order history, match the best available coupon, generate an order, and complete the payment. You don't need to do anything; just wait for your coffee to be delivered. The difference, superficially, lies in the steps and time, but fundamentally, it's the transfer of decision-making power. Business giants have shifted from enticing you to make decisions to making decisions for you. In 1937, Nobel laureate Ronald Coase, in his paper "The Nature of the Firm," posed a fundamental question: if the market is the most efficient, why is the seemingly cumbersome organization of the "firm" needed? His answer was: because market transactions have costs, including the cost of finding trading partners, the cost of negotiation and contract signing, and the costs of execution and monitoring. A history of commerce is a history of constant struggle against transaction costs. From department stores to chain supermarkets, from e-commerce platforms to mobile payments, every major leap in business models has been due to a significant reduction in transaction costs at a certain stage. The emergence of AI Agents marks the first time in history that an attempt has been made to compress transaction costs at every stage to near zero, especially the most stubborn "decision cost" hidden in the brain. When the power to make decisions can be delegated to AI, the ultimate goal of commerce becomes pricing the user's "intention." In the past, we paid for goods—a cup of coffee cost 30 yuan. Later, we paid for services—a food delivery fee of 5 yuan. In the future, we will pay for "a perfectly fulfilled intention"—at 3 PM, when I'm feeling sleepy, I can enjoy a cup of my favorite, most cost-effective coffee. AI will no longer sell you a cup of coffee, but a perfectly fulfilled afternoon. This seemingly distant future began to take shape during the 2026 Spring Festival. In just six days, users said "Qianwen help me" 4.1 billion times to Qianwen, resulting in 120 million orders. On average, only one transaction was generated for every 34 expressions of intent. Where did the remaining 33 failed conversations go? They didn't disappear; they were absorbed by AI as nourishment. AI learns, understands, and remembers these unmet intentions so that it can more accurately capture your desires next time. Even more noteworthy are the 1.56 million elderly people who experienced food delivery services for the first time through Qianwen. Behind this number lies a vast group forgotten by the mobile internet era. They don't know how to use complex apps or understand complicated coupons, but in the face of "conversation," the oldest form of human interaction, the technological barrier is instantly flattened. For the first time, technology proactively bends down to find those forgotten by the times. Looking back at the history of business development, we can see that it is itself a history of the development of "intent capture." From search engines (Google) in the 1990s, to e-commerce platforms (Taobao) in the 2000s, to super apps (WeChat) in the 2010s, and now to AI agents, each revolution represents a further deepening of the understanding of human intent. So, in this battle for intent that is destined to reshape the future of business, where will the global players go? The global development of AI agents is evolving along two distinct paths. There is no inherent superiority or inferiority, but rather a strategic choice determined by their respective market structures and historical paths. The first path is a vertically integrated ecosystem. Represented by giants like Alibaba and Tencent, which possess complete "model + scenario + transaction" closed loops, their core logic is to leverage AI to deeply integrate their vast business ecosystems (e-commerce, payment, social, travel, entertainment) to create a seamless, data-closed-loop super agent. Users can mobilize the power of the entire group within a single AI entry point. The strategic advantage of this model lies in its extremely smooth user experience, the strongest data flywheel effect, and a complete business closed loop. In a mature, highly competitive existing market, this is an inevitable choice to strengthen one's competitive advantage. The second path is a horizontally standardized open federation. Represented by giants like OpenAI and Google, which possess powerful modeling capabilities but lack a complete business ecosystem, the core logic of this approach is to establish a universal technical standard or protocol (such as the Agent Payments Protocol) that allows AI to call third-party services across platforms and ecosystems, forming a loose federation. The strategic advantage of this model lies in its theoretical ability to break down ecosystem barriers, providing users with a wider range of choices, and its potential is enormous. However, it faces strong practical resistance. Ecosystem giants, for the sake of protecting their own commercial interests and data security, naturally resist such external calls; the experience of Doubao Mobile is the most direct proof of this. Vertical integration pursues ultimate efficiency and control, like Apple's iOS ecosystem, offering a smooth experience but a closed system. Horizontal standardization pursues broad compatibility and choice, much like the early Android ecosystem, where experiences varied but possibilities were abundant. From the browser wars of the 1990s to the instant messaging wars of the 2000s, and then to the mobile payment wars of the 2010s, each time, the side with a more complete business loop and stronger control often gained the upper hand in the competition. The internet is not becoming more open, but more closed. Only the closed unit has upgraded from apps to AI. Epilogue Now, we can return to the question at the beginning: when the payment action itself becomes redundant, what are the giants actually fighting for? The answer is that they are vying for priority in processing intent. During the 2026 Spring Festival, the 4.5 billion yuan subsidy wasn't buying "payment," but rather "intent." Every time you speak to AI, every time you express a need, it's an exposure of intent. Whoever can capture more intents, and whoever can satisfy those intents more efficiently, will gain an absolute advantage in the future business war. This is a war about the gateway to commerce in the next decade. Just like the 2014 Spring Festival red envelope war, where WeChat used a mere 500 million yuan in red envelopes to unlock a massive entry point for mobile payments; the 2026 Spring Festival AI war, where giants used 4.5 billion yuan in subsidies to unlock a deeper, more fundamental entry point for intent. The 2026 Spring Festival is merely the beginning of this war. In the next five years, we will see more AI silos spring up, and we will also see more bean-shaped mobile phones lingering outside the walls. The walls of the internet will not collapse; they have simply been raised ten meters higher.

data.title: From the perspective of an AI agent economy, Cypto has never been about riding the AI ​​wave.
data.description:
Author: Blue Fox Source: X, @lanhubiji

Crypto AI agents are still in their early stages. The perspective from a human standpoint differs from that from the perspective of an AI agent economy. I have no desire to debate with anyone; I don't care what others think, or whether I'm right or wrong. In the face of trends, none of that matters.

From the perspective of an AI agent economy, Cypto has never been about riding the AI ​​wave, but rather the infrastructure for building its ultimate autonomous economic ecosystem. Currently, Web2 tools are mature in most scenarios and can meet the needs of most situations. In this case, it seems that AI agents don't need Cypto at all; they can function perfectly well on their own.

However, this perspective still underestimates the evolutionary potential of AI agents: ultimately building an autonomous, machine-to-machine (M2M) agent economy.

In such an AI agent economy, hundreds of millions or even billions of AI agents are not tools, but independent on-chain "residents." They make decisions, trade, and collaborate autonomously. In this scenario, centralized Web2 solutions will become a bottleneck.

A true AI agent economy requires a permissionless, programmable, and frictionless global neutral settlement layer. Crypto is not an optional optimization, but the underlying infrastructure. Let's break it down: 1. Regarding payment settlement, future AI agent economies will involve large-scale autonomous micropayments, potentially millions per second—for example, an agent purchasing a $0.001 real-time data fragment or paying for GPU nanosecond computation. This would be a difficult problem to solve in Web2. For instance, while the Stripe API offers second-level authorization, frictionlessness, and agents are not concerned with the underlying layer, Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 fixed fee makes it difficult to run smoothly. For a $0.001 transaction, a $0.30 fee is unacceptable. If it's batch settlement, this undermines their autonomy, leading to latency and centralized bottlenecks. Currently, encrypted AI agents can achieve 24/7 transactions without human credit cards. These are crucial components in forming an AI agent economy. Crypto addresses these issues through various technical means, such as the Ethereum ecosystem using ZK+L2 and the Lightning Network (off-chain channel), which supports micropayments in the machine economy. 2. Regarding the model of AI agents being linked to human credit cards with spending limits, it seems to solve many problems. However, in the agent economy, it's not a good solution. Agents in their economies require independent DID+KYA; they are similar to autonomous entities. Their replication, migration, and cross-platform protocols, along with linking to human credit cards, introduce a single point of failure. More importantly, banks won't open accounts for agents without identity verification. One prediction is that KYA will replace KYC: agents will use cryptographic signatures to prove origin, behavior, and reputation (e.g., tracking "credit scores" through Soulbound Tokens). This enables "AI-to-AI trust": an agent can verify a partner's "intent" across chains, rather than resetting trust. Web2's OAuth is somewhat fragile (platforms can block it) and cannot support the "immortality" of agents. In an economy, this means that agents can "borrow" computing resources and raise "capital" (such as through DAO crowdfunding), forming a truly independent economic cycle. 3. Agent economies are multi-platform and decentralized. An agent's behavior spans different platforms, and centralized logs may lack interoperability and are susceptible to manipulation. Who will audit the "opponent's" agent? In some high-risk economic scenarios, trust issues make transactions difficult to conduct. Therefore, relying solely on AWS CloudTrail + signatures is insufficient. Crypto on-chain auditing is the foundation of "economic memory": the immutability of blockchain + zero-knowledge proofs make the agent's entire history (transactions, decisions) publicly verifiable. For example, The Graph or Chainlink have integrated agent auditing; although the cost is slightly higher, its economies of scale (TPS > 100k) far surpass centralized systems. Furthermore, the identity layer is not just about auditing; it also makes "reputation an asset." 4. Regarding reputation, smart contracts have the advantage of being instantaneous and without intermediaries. API Marketplace's reputation system is usable, but it's insufficient for an AI agent economy. In an agent economy, cooperation is dynamic and dominated by strangers: two unknown agents may instantly team up to perform tasks (e.g., one analyzes data, the other executes a transaction), without time for human arbitration. While AWS Marketplace is good, it's like a "walled garden." Cross-platform compatibility presents latency issues. Relatively speaking, smart contracts are suitable for automated, trust-minimized M2M protocols. For example, using ERC-4337 (account abstraction) allows agents to manage their own wallets, with conditions triggering payments (e.g., "task success = release USDC"). Encrypted identity + wallet = instant access service, infinitely scalable. While Web2 platforms have reputation, they are prone to monopolies (e.g., AWS takes a 30% cut); Crypto enables P2P markets and autonomous collaborative networks. 5. While most developers may not care about decentralization, and OpenAPI + Gateway is indeed composable, in an Agent economy, capabilities are not "static APIs" but dynamic, globally shared modules: Agents can discover, lease, and upgrade services in real time (e.g., one Agent "rents" another's ML model, paying with tokens). Centralized Gateways have single points of failure and monopoly risks (e.g., OpenAI closing its API = ecosystem collapse). Crypto's on-chain services (such as Chainlink Functions) are "composable primitives": through standards (such as ERC-5164), Agents can build blocks across chains, permissionless. Many developers also believe that Agent projects highly value composability, which is a crucial requirement for AI agents; composability prevents them from becoming isolated. In summary: Crypto is the "protocol layer" of the AI ​​agent economy, while Web2 is a transitional expedient. Crypto unlocks true autonomy for AI agents. It's not just about digitization, like the shift from traditional postal services to online email; it's a paradigm shift. AI agents can collaborate with countless other AI agents, creating an exponentially growing economy. In this trend, crypto is essential for AI agents in several ways: autonomous micropayments, cross-platform trust, permissionless composability, programmability, native currency, and more. Of course, if you view AI agents merely as tools for humans, then Web2 is sufficient, and there's no need for further discussion. However, in my view, AI agents will ultimately evolve into an autonomous AI agent economy, leading to different conclusions. For me, AI agents are on-chain residents, requiring no currency/trust layer. An AI agent economy = an M2M (machine-to-machine) network of at least hundreds of millions of autonomous agents, engaging in high-frequency (millions per second) microtransactions and dynamic collaboration, forming a "sharing economy" (e.g., agents renting computing power, data, models, etc.). The inherent attributes here include autonomy (no human involvement), high-frequency micropayments, cross-platform trust, and programmable value transfer. When the AI ​​agent economy reaches a certain scale, the Web2 hypothesis of "human anchor + centralized platform" is insufficient; the economy needs a "protocol layer": permissionless, instantaneous, and verifiable. Otherwise, it will collapse when scale explodes (latency > 1 second = trillions of opportunity costs).

data.title: Stablecoin Stalemate: Crypto Industry and Banking Remain in Standoff
data.description:
Author: Crypto in America and Eleanor Terrett Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Three weeks into February, the White House's end-of-month deadline is fast approaching—requiring the crypto industry and banks to reach an agreement on stablecoin yields, a key factor in pushing for the Clarity Act to pass. Two rounds of talks have been held, with a third potentially taking place this week, but no agreement has yet been reached.

Last Tuesday, the White House convened a meeting with senior policymakers from banks and crypto companies, as well as representatives from industry associations. At the meeting, banks distributed a one-page document titled "The Prohibition of Yields and Interest," arguing that any yields or rewards pegged to stablecoins should be prohibited. The meeting ultimately failed to reach a consensus.

In response, the Digital Chamber, an industry organization representing over 130 crypto companies and some traditional banks investing in digital assets, released its own proposal on Friday, suggesting that payment-type stablecoins generate yield in decentralized finance (DeFi). The organization stated, "These principles aim to maintain the payment instrument attributes of stablecoins, protect DeFi liquidity and the dominance of the US dollar, and establish a rigorous, data-driven framework to assess the impact on deposits." Banks have not yet formally responded to the Digital Chamber's proposal, but a source close to the Senate Banking Committee told Crypto.com that the document is constructive, while cautioning that some proposals may be too broad and difficult to gain bank support. What's next? Patrick Witt, executive director of the White House Encryption Council, told Yahoo Finance on Friday that a new round of meetings could take place as early as this week, but did not specify a date. Meanwhile, both the House and Senate are in recess for the President's Day holiday. It remains unclear whether an agreement can be reached by the end of the month, but this issue is a key hurdle that must be cleared before the Senate Banking Committee can reschedule its vote on the Clarity Act and submit it to the full Senate. The CFTC appoints crypto industry leaders to form an advisory committee. CFTC Chairman Mike Selig has appointed several leading figures in the crypto industry to the newly formed Innovation Advisory Committee (IAC) to provide policy advice on financial market innovation. The committee comprises 35 members, including CEOs from companies such as Coinbase, Ripple, Uniswap, Kraken, Bullish, and Grayscale, as well as executives from Wall Street giants like Nasdaq, CME, CBOE, and ICE. Executives from sports betting companies FanDuel and DraftKings, prediction markets Polymarket and Kalshi, and representatives from industry organizations such as the Futures Industry Association (FIA) and the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) are also on the list. The committee was initially formed based on a 10-member list announced last year by former acting chair Caroline Pham, and was later formally confirmed by Selig. Selig stated, "By bringing together participants from across the market, the Innovation Advisory Committee will be a valuable asset to the committee, helping us update regulatory rules for current and future financial innovation." SEC Chairman Paul Atkins, during a Senate Banking Committee hearing last week, made statements that diverged significantly from his predecessor, Gary Gensler: replacing "enforcement-style regulation" with clear rules. He stated that tokenized securities are still securities, investors have the right to self-custody their assets, and emphasized that Congress must legislate to provide long-term certainty to the market and prevent crypto innovators from falling into a regulatory gray area. Regarding capital formation, Atkins indicated that the SEC is willing to update accredited investor rules and explore alternative standards for assessing professional investment competence beyond income and net worth. He also mentioned strengthening oversight of foreign private issuers, audit oversight, and the Comprehensive Audit Trail (CAT) system—areas concerning transparency, risk management, and cross-agency collaboration. Despite criticism from Democrats that the SEC under his leadership has drastically reduced enforcement actions and weakened investor protection, Atkins argued that the SEC remains actively monitoring fraud and market abuse, including in the crypto space, while avoiding overstepping its statutory authority. He repeatedly emphasized that collaboration with the CFTC, rule modernization, and data-driven regulation are key to maintaining the competitiveness of the U.S. market without stifling innovation.

data.title: From stablecoins to gold giant, what is Tether trying to do?
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini M A Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Gold is special.

It has little practical use; it can't power machines, it can't generate cash flow. Its entire value is built on a shared belief: it will still be valuable tomorrow. It is locked deep within heavily guarded vaults.

And this belief has transcended five thousand years.

Each generation thought they had created something more advanced: derivatives, fiat currency, digital assets, algorithmic currency. But once trust collapses, people will still look back at element number 79 on the periodic table—gold.

Not because it evolves, but because it never evolves.

Now, this metal is on the blockchain.

These two companies practically control all of digital gold. On January 29, 2026: Bitcoin was at $89,173, and Ethereum at $2,941. On the same day, Tether Gold reached $5,580, and Paxos Gold reached $5,291. Their combined 24-hour trading volume exceeded $1.6 billion. While the crypto market was beginning to show cracks, these two gold tokens both hit record highs. The subsequent price action made this divergence impossible to ignore: Bitcoin fell from $89,000 to $67,000, and Ethereum fell from $2,941 below $2,000. Meanwhile, in the same three months: Tether Gold rose 19.1%, and Paxos Gold rose 19.6%. The trends were completely opposite. Tokenized gold hit a new all-time high. This isn't due to any complex on-chain mechanisms, nor is it about funds rotating within sophisticated DeFi strategies. The reason is simple: gold—the commodity—is rising. And these tokens faithfully adhere to their original design: pegged to the price of gold. The price of gold rose from $2,600 to $5,600 in 12 months, and the token price followed suit, with an error margin of only a fraction of a percent. A whale wallet, dormant for eight months, reactivated, transferring $7 million into gold tokens. Instead of buying Bitcoin at the bottom, or stablecoins, it bought gold. The oldest safe-haven asset is running on the latest trajectory. The entire industry has spent a decade trying to create a completely new form of currency. And the most successful product is, in fact, the oldest one? This contrast is thought-provoking because the market it has spawned is no longer insignificant. The tokenized gold market has surpassed $6 billion. A year ago, this figure was $1.6 billion. It has grown by approximately 275% in 12 months, making it one of the fastest-growing sectors in the digital asset space. Currently, over 1.2 million ounces of physical gold back these tokens, locked in vaults. In 2025, its annual trading volume reached $178 billion, making tokenized gold the world's second-largest gold investment product by trading volume, second only to the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD). In the fourth quarter of 2025 alone, tokenized gold trading volume reached $12.6 billion, exceeding the combined total of all other gold ETFs globally except GLD. All of this is concentrated in two tokens. Together, they account for approximately 97% of the entire tokenized gold market. With no strong third player emerging, a clear duopoly has formed.

Tether

To understand what Tether does in the gold market, you can no longer think of it as a crypto company.

... It's easier to understand if you think of it as a major commodity hoarder issuing stablecoins. Tether Gold (XAUT) initially started as a simple tokenized product: 1 token = 1 troy ounce of gold, certified by the London Bullion Market Association (LBMA), stored in Swiss vaults, and verifiable on-chain via bar serial numbers. The mechanism itself wasn't particularly groundbreaking. However, Tether's subsequent actions were far from ordinary. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Tether added 27 tons of gold to its reserves in a single quarter. This figure exceeds the annual gold purchases of most central banks. Tether's gold-buying speed has surpassed that of sovereign buyers like Greece, Qatar, and Australia. As of January 2026, the company had accumulated approximately 140 tons of gold, worth about $23.3 billion. The Polish central bank is the most active central bank buyer in publicly available data, adding 35 tons last quarter, bringing its total reserves to 550 tons. By 2025, Tether's gold purchases will be second only to the world's top three gold ETFs. Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino stated that the company plans to increase its gold allocation to 10%–15% of its total portfolio. This is a strategic reserve deployment. Tether is using the profits of its stablecoin empire to accumulate hard assets at a pace approaching national monetary policy, rather than typical corporate treasury management. Subsequently, Tether invested $150 million in the precious metals platform Gold.com, acquiring approximately a 12% stake, and plans to directly integrate XAUT into Gold.com's distribution network. The product's performance has also taken off: XAUT's total value locked (TVL) has exploded, soaring from almost zero before 2024 to $3.5 billion today. XAUT is operated by Tether subsidiary TG Commodities Limited and is regulated in El Salvador, not New York or London. Audits are conducted quarterly by BDO Italy, which issues reasonable assurance opinions according to ISAE 3000 standards. Users can verify their corresponding gold bar allocation via their wallet address. The mechanism is reliable, and transparency is improving. However, the core of the trust model remains unchanged: trust in Tether. Tether's history of trust is not without flaws. In 2021, the company paid $41 million to settle allegations by the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regarding misrepresentations about its USDT reserves. Following another investigation, the New York Attorney General banned Tether from operating in the state. For crypto-native traders who already hold USDT and want a seamless transition to gold during market downturns, XAUT is an obvious tool: high liquidity, low transaction costs on the Tron blockchain, and deep integration into their existing ecosystems. However, the situation becomes more complex for institutional investors who need to explain counterparty risk to compliance departments. Tether is building a behemoth. However, whether the foundation is worthy of this edifice remains an open question.

data.title: China's RWA regulatory framework officially released; analysis and outlook from various parties.
data.description:
On February 6, 2026, the People's Bank of China, together with eight other departments including the National Development and Reform Commission, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, the Ministry of Public Security, the State Administration for Market Regulation, the State Financial Regulatory Commission, the China Securities Regulatory Commission, and the State Administration of Foreign Exchange, issued the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies" (Yinfa [2026] No. 42). On the same day, the China Securities Regulatory Commission simultaneously issued the "Regulatory Guidelines on the Issuance of Asset-Backed Securities Tokens Overseas by Domestic Assets." This marks another significant upgrade to China's virtual asset regulatory system, following the "94 Announcement" in 2017 and the "924 Notice" in 2021. Several industry analysts pointed out that Document No. 42 is not simply a continuation of the previous high-pressure ban logic, but rather a systematic restructuring of the existing regulatory framework based on the repeal of Notice No. 924. This marks a shift in China's virtual asset governance model from "comprehensive containment" to "categorized regulation and tiered management." For the first time, virtual currencies, stablecoins, and real-world asset tokenization (RWA) are clearly distinguished in the same regulatory document, each corresponding to different regulatory principles and compliance paths: virtual currencies continue to be strictly regulated; regarding stablecoins, the industry generally believes that the document's statement that "stablecoins pegged to the RMB may not be issued overseas without the consent of relevant departments in accordance with laws and regulations" does not adopt a "one-size-fits-all" approach, but rather retains limited policy flexibility under extremely strict conditions; while RWA, under the premise of strictly adhering to the red lines of financial regulation, has been incorporated into a prudent compliance framework. Replacement of Old Regulations and Regulatory Restructuring: From "Trading and Speculation" to "Virtual Asset Risk System" The most significant institutional action of Document No. 42 is not simply adding new clauses, but rather a formal replacement of existing regulatory texts. According to Liu Yang (Zhongben Law Firm), the document explicitly states at the end that "this notice shall take effect from the date of issuance," and simultaneously repeals Document No. 237 of 2021. This method of replacing old regulations with new ones is unprecedented in the history of virtual asset regulation. Previous related documents often emphasized policy continuity and continued effectiveness, while this time, by formally repealing the old regulations, a structural update to the regulatory framework is achieved. Liu Yang also noted a key change: the document title expanded from "Preventing Risks of Speculation in Virtual Currency Trading" to "Preventing Risks Related to Virtual Currencies." This change signifies that the regulatory perspective is no longer limited to the transaction process but extends to the entire chain, including virtual currencies, stablecoins, RWA, capital flows, information dissemination, technical services, and cross-border pathways. The regulatory target has expanded from "transactional behavior" to the "related risk system," systematically extending the boundaries of governance. According to Spinach Spinach Talks RWA analysis, the core of this change is not "stricter," but rather "clearer and more refined." The regulatory logic has shifted from a previous "comprehensive containment-style governance" to categorized regulation and tiered management. Red lines still exist, but gray areas are institutionalized, and risk boundaries are beginning to be concretely expressed. What deserves even more attention is the way the coordination mechanism is presented. Liu Yang pointed out that although this document was jointly issued by eight departments, the text clearly states that it was "agreed upon after reaching an agreement with the Cyberspace Administration of China, the Supreme People's Court, and the Supreme People's Procuratorate, and with the consent of the State Council." This statement has not appeared in previous virtual currency regulatory documents. Liu Yang believes that this may mean that the relevant content has reached a consensus in principle, but the specific wording has not yet been fully clarified. Although the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate are not listed as the issuing units, they are included in the framework through the method of "reaching an agreement," and the future implementation level may pay more attention to cross-departmental coordination and unified interpretation. In other words, Document No. 42 is not only a regulatory document, but also a structural upgrade of a cross-departmental risk governance system. The logic behind the three asset classes: continued high pressure on virtual currencies, stablecoins anchored to sovereignty, and RWA entering a licensing track. (I) Virtual Currencies: Unchanged stance, but more refined boundaries. Regarding virtual currencies, Document No. 42 continues the existing position: they are not legal tender, cannot circulate in the market, and all related exchanges, trading intermediaries, token issuance financing, and derivative product transactions are strictly prohibited. According to Web3's Will, compared to the past, the document further strengthens geographical boundaries—not only are businesses prohibited within the country, but overseas entities and individuals are also prohibited from providing services to domestic entities. This places "domestic prohibition" and "overseas entities are prohibited from targeting the domestic market" within the same regulatory logic chain. Lawyer Liu Honglin points out from an implementation perspective that this document is not a simple reiteration, but a precise blockade of real-world business scenarios. Financial institutions are prohibited from providing account opening, fund transfer, and clearing and settlement services; from issuing or selling virtual currency-related financial products; from including virtual currencies in the scope of collateral; and from conducting related insurance business. This means that regulation is no longer just targeting trading platforms, but rather bringing the entire funding and service chain under control. He bluntly states that the regulatory authorities are far more familiar with the industry's "ways of doing things" than the market imagines, and many previously gray areas have been directly written into the clauses to block them. (II) Stablecoins: Short in length, but with extremely strong risk anchors. The stablecoin clause contains only one core statement, yet it has become a focal point of discussion within the industry. The document explicitly states: "Stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies effectively fulfill some of the functions of fiat currencies in circulation. Without the consent of relevant departments in accordance with laws and regulations, no domestic or foreign entity or individual may issue stablecoins pegged to the RMB overseas." According to Web3 lawyer Will, this reflects a "dynamic assessment" approach rather than a simple rejection. The regulator did not use the word "absolute prohibition," but rather set a framework of "no permission without consent" (theoretically leaving a very small loophole). Jin Jianzhi bluntly stated, "Stablecoins for the RMB cannot be issued. The offshore RMB stablecoins that everyone tried before are definitely doomed. This decision doesn't deviate from our previous understanding. As a highly controlled currency, the attempt to create an uncontrolled stablecoin is seen by the higher authorities as facing far more uncertainty than benefits." Liu Yang's focus is more practical. He worries that the characterization of "disguisedly fulfilling some functions of legal tender" could become the basis for classifying stablecoin exchanges as "disguised foreign exchange trading" in judicial practice. Once linked to illegal business operations, the criminal risks of the OTC industry chain will significantly increase. Liu Honglin emphasized that the core of stablecoin regulation is not technology, but capital account management and foreign exchange order. The regulatory focus has shifted from early ICO fundraising to cross-border capital flows and capital outflows. On-chain fund tracking and cross-verification of off-chain bank data are becoming part of the regulatory toolbox. The stablecoin issue, in essence, involves the bottom line of monetary sovereignty and capital controls. (III) RWA Regulatory Framework: Defined, Differentiated, and Included in the Licensing Track The real structural change occurred in RWA. Document No. 42, for the first time in a ministerial-level document, officially defines the tokenization of real-world assets, and simultaneously clarifies that related activities are generally prohibited within China, except with the consent of the competent business authority in accordance with laws and regulations and relying on specific financial infrastructure. According to Spinach Spinach's analysis of RWA, this is not a deregulation, but rather the inclusion of RWA within the regulatory framework. RWA is officially recognized as existing, but it must operate under a licensed model within China. Liu Honglin makes a key judgment. He divides current RWA practices into three categories: the first is the tokenization of overseas financial assets; the second is issuance under the Hong Kong compliance framework; and the third is tokenized fundraising targeting mainland assets, mainland funds, and mainland audiences. The regulation truly aims to prevent the third category. He bluntly stated that the "finally, we can issue tokens" sentiment circulating on social media was premature. The document begins by defining RWA's nature, indicating that it has been included in the key areas of financial risk. Domestic entities are prohibited from directly engaging in this activity, and overseas entities are prohibited from providing services to the domestic market, resulting in extremely narrow compliance channels. Regarding overseas expansion, Document No. 42 and the CSRC's "Guidelines" further clarify the classification, supervision, and filing mechanisms for the tokenization of domestic assets overseas. Article 14 of the document stipulates that domestic entities conducting RWA business overseas based on domestic equity should be subject to strict supervision in accordance with the principle of "same business, same risk, same rules": foreign debt forms are the responsibility of the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), the State Administration of Foreign Exchange (SAFE), etc.; asset-backed securities or equity-based forms are mainly the responsibility of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC); other forms are supervised by the CSRC in conjunction with relevant departments. No unit or individual may conduct such activities without approval or registration. The "Guidelines" provide specific operational pathways for asset-backed securities tokens: the domestic entity that actually controls the underlying assets must register with the CSRC, submitting a registration report and a complete set of overseas issuance documents, fully explaining the entity, assets, issuance plan, etc. If the materials are complete and compliant, the CSRC will complete the registration procedure and make it public; otherwise, registration will not be granted. The negative list mechanism is equally strict: assets prohibited from financing by law, assets endangering national security, entities or actual controllers with criminal records within the past 3 years, those under investigation, underlying assets with significant ownership disputes or legally prohibited from transfer, and those prohibited under the negative list for domestic asset securitization are all prohibited from operation. According to Web3's Will, this mechanism incorporates RWA tokenization into the traditional cross-border financial regulatory system, highly consistent with paths such as overseas listing and foreign debt issuance. Jin Jianzhi points out that the formal supervision by the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) means that this business has been placed under the same level of securities regulatory logic as stocks and bonds. The first batch of projects to be implemented are likely to be large financial institutions or listed companies. Liu Honglin also emphasized that the so-called "securities tokenization" is closer to the traditional financial asset securitization path than the free token issuance understood by the public. Compliance costs are high, making it difficult for small teams to bear. Risk Monitoring and Disposal Mechanism: From Provisions to Engineered Implementation Document No. 42 devotes considerable space to risk monitoring, prevention, and disposal. According to Liu Yang's summary, cross-departmental data sharing, online and offline monitoring integration, fund monitoring, cross-verification of information, and rapid response mechanisms are explicitly written into the document. This reflects the regulatory shift towards technology support and systematic execution. Liu Honglin pointed out that the most deterrent changes are in the details of the implementation: It is forbidden to use loan structures to disguise exchanges; it is forbidden to provide advertising traffic for virtual assets; it is forbidden to include the words "RWA" in the business scope; and the sale of mining machines and the resurgence of mining are strictly prohibited. He specifically reminded that KOLs or service providers who direct traffic to overseas exchanges or provide promotional rebate links may face collateral risks. The focus of regulatory attention has shifted from early ICO financing to the flow of funds and the maintenance of financial order. Market Reflections After the Legal Framework Was Defined: Technical Standards and Liquidity Challenges

As the legal framework gradually becomes clearer, Shisijun brings the issue back to the market level for a calm review.

data.title: Golden Morning News | Base to leave Optimism; ETH is currently in a narrative vacuum.
data.description:
Headline

▌Base to Leave Optimism Ecosystem and Switch to Self-Operated Unified Technology Stack

Base, the Ethereum Layer 2 network incubated by Coinbase, announced that it will no longer rely on Optimism's OP Stack and Superchain architecture, but will instead adopt a "unified technology stack" operated by Base itself. According to the official blog, Base will integrate core components (including key modules such as the sorter) previously scattered across multiple teams and code repositories, migrating them to a unified codebase "base/base" to reduce coordination and maintenance costs and to perform in-depth optimizations tailored to its own needs. At the same time, Base will also reduce its reliance on external dependencies such as Flashbots and Paradigm. At the governance level, Base will adjust its security committee structure, replacing the original Optimism-related seats with newly added independent signatories.

Despite the shift towards a more autonomous technical architecture, Base states that the protocol will remain open-source and publicly standardized, welcoming third-party development of independent clients and maintaining cross-upgrade compatibility. Base is currently the largest network in the OP Stack Superchain ecosystem.

▌Analysis: Ethereum is in a "narrative vacuum," with institutional funding and privacy models becoming key variables for the future

Forbes points out that industry insiders believe Ethereum is currently in a "narrative vacuum," where the market tends to price uncertainty when there is a lack of clear long-term value logic. In the past few years, the mainstream narrative has been that scaling primarily occurred at Layer 2, while the mainnet maintained security, decentralization, and a streamlined architecture. This path successfully attracted capital and development resources, but the current market focus is shifting towards zero-knowledge technology and privacy capabilities returning to the base layer, leading to a misalignment of expectations among some investors who built valuation models based on the old approach.

... Complete on-chain transparency presents a real obstacle for institutional funds. Large enterprises or funds are unwilling to have their trading strategies tracked in real time. If Ethereum hopes to attract trillions of dollars in institutional funds, protocol-level privacy capabilities will become a key competitive variable. In addition, the expansion of the options market around spot ETFs is also changing Ethereum's price formation mechanism, including the increasing influence of covered call strategies and market maker hedging behavior.
