    ▌What are Long and Short Positions?

    Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline. Cryptocurrency traders and investors utilize these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and to capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions.

    In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (usually borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: Just revealed – Buffett's "final work"
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway continued to adjust its holdings in tech giants in the final quarter before Warren Buffett stepped down as CEO, reducing its holdings in several major stocks while establishing its first position in traditional media outlet The New York Times.

On Tuesday, February 17th, after the US stock market closed, a 13F filing submitted to the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) disclosed that Berkshire Hathaway significantly reduced its holdings in Amazon (AMZN) by more than 77% in the fourth quarter of 2025, and reduced its holdings in Apple (AAPL) for the third consecutive quarter. Berkshire Hathaway also continued to sell off its stake in Bank of America (BAC), reducing its stake in the bank to just below 7%.

The New York Times (NYT) was Berkshire Hathaway's only new addition to its portfolio in the fourth quarter, with over 5.06 million shares acquired, valued at approximately $352 million. Following the announcement, the New York Times' stock price initially rose over 10% in after-hours trading, before recovering more than half of its gains. This Tuesday's release was Buffett's final 13F filing during his 60-year tenure at Berkshire Hathaway. His successor, Greg Abel, will officially assume the CEO position on January 1, 2026. Berkshire Hathaway will release its full-year results on February 28, at which time it will also publish Abel's first letter to shareholders as CEO. According to its 13F filings, Berkshire Hathaway sold over 10.29 million Apple shares in the fourth quarter, a 4.3% decrease from the third quarter. The market value of its Apple holdings decreased by approximately $2.8 billion at the end of the quarter, and its proportion in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio fell from 22.69% in the third quarter to 22.60%. In terms of the decrease in market value compared to the previous quarter, Apple was the stock that Berkshire Hathaway reduced its holdings the most in the fourth quarter, but the reduction was less than in the previous quarter. In the third quarter, Berkshire Hathaway reduced its Apple holdings by approximately 41.79 million shares, a decrease of over 14.9% compared to the previous quarter. Despite the continued reduction, Apple remained Berkshire Hathaway's largest holding by the end of the fourth quarter. Bank of America was another major holding that Berkshire Hathaway continued to reduce its holdings in. In the fourth quarter, Berkshire Hathaway sold nearly 50.8 million Bank of America shares, a decrease of 8.9% compared to the previous quarter, reducing its holdings to 6.89%. By the end of the fourth quarter, Bank of America remained Berkshire Hathaway's third-largest holding. However, Berkshire's holdings in Bank of America had decreased from 1.03 billion shares in mid-2024 to 517 million shares, representing a reduction of approximately 50% over the past year and a half. Amazon holdings plummeted by 77% in the fourth quarter. Based on changes in holding percentage, Amazon was the stock with the largest reduction in Berkshire's holdings in the fourth quarter. According to the 13F filing, Amazon's share in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio plummeted to 0.19% in the fourth quarter, down 0.63 percentage points from the third quarter, with the number of shares decreasing to approximately 2.3 million, a drop of over 77.2% quarter-over-quarter. Berkshire first bought Amazon in 2019. At the time, Buffett stated that while he has historically been cautious about tech stocks, not buying the online retail giant's stock earlier would have been "foolish." Amazon's stock price has fallen approximately 11% since the beginning of 2026, experiencing its largest year-over-year decline in 20 years. On Tuesday, Amazon ended its longest losing streak since 2006, lasting nine trading days. In the nine trading days ending last Friday, Amazon's market capitalization evaporated by over $450 billion, reflecting investor skepticism about the rationale behind Amazon's massive AI-related spending. When Amazon released its fourth-quarter earnings report on the 5th of this month, it revealed plans for $200 billion in capital expenditures by 2026, exceeding the annual investment of any other tech giant.

Established a position in The New York Times

The New York Times was the only stock Berkshire Hathaway established a new position in during the fourth quarter. During the quarter, Berkshire Hathaway purchased 5.067 million shares, with a market value of over $352 million, holding approximately 3.1% of The New York Times' shares. This position ranks 30th in Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio, representing 0.13%.

Berkshire Hathaway held media stocks for a long time, but sold its newspaper business to Lee Enterprises for $140 million in 2020. Warren Buffett was the largest outside shareholder and board member of The Washington Post. The New York Times stock has recently performed strongly, rising 6% year-to-date and currently near its all-time high of $74.59, with a cumulative increase of 50% over the past 12 months. Berkshire Hathaway significantly increased its holdings in oil giants Chevron (CVX) and Chubb Insurance in the fourth quarter. Specifically, Berkshire increased its holdings in Chubb Insurance (CB) by nearly 2.92 million shares, a 9.3% increase quarter-over-quarter. CB's share in the portfolio rose from 3.31% in the third quarter to 3.90%, an increase of 0.59 percentage points quarter-over-quarter. In terms of the increase in holdings percentage, Chubb Insurance saw the largest increase in holdings in the fourth quarter. Berkshire Hathaway purchased over 8.09 million shares of Chevron in the fourth quarter, representing a 6.6% increase in its holdings compared to the previous quarter. Its shareholding percentage increased by 0.15 percentage points to 7.24%, with a market value increase of approximately $1.23 billion. In terms of the increase in market value, Chevron saw the largest increase in holdings in the fourth quarter. Berkshire Hathaway also increased its holdings of Domino's Pizza (DPZ) by 368,000 shares in the fourth quarter, representing a 12.3% increase in its holdings compared to the previous quarter, bringing its total holdings to approximately $1.4 billion, ranking 20th. Berkshire Hathaway's holding in Kraft Heinz, another consumer staples stock, remained unchanged in the fourth quarter. Abel recently stated that he supports Kraft Heinz's decision to suspend the spin-off of its condiments and grocery businesses. Kraft Heinz disclosed in a January regulatory filing that its largest shareholder, Berkshire Hathaway, might sell nearly all of its 325.6 million shares. Buffett told the Wall Street Journal last year that Berkshire Hathaway did not support the spin-off plan. According to SEC regulations, institutional investors managing at least $100 million in U.S. stocks must file a 13F filing within 45 days of the end of each quarter. Although these documents provide outdated holdings information, Berkshire Hathaway's disclosures are still widely interpreted by market participants seeking insights into the company's investment decisions. By the end of the fourth quarter, in terms of market value, Berkshire Hathaway's top ten holdings were largely the same as the previous quarter, with most rankings unchanged from the third quarter, except for Moody's and Occidental Petroleum, whose rankings had swapped. Similar to the third quarter, only four of these heavily weighted stocks saw adjustments in holdings in the fourth quarter: Apple and American Express were reduced, while Chevron and Chubb were increased. Alphabet, Google's parent company, which entered the top ten for the first time in the third quarter, remains in tenth place. DVA, a kidney dialysis service provider that was pushed out of the top ten by Alphabet in the third quarter, remains in eleventh place in the fourth quarter. Berkshire Hathaway's top ten holdings in the fourth quarter were: Apple (AAPL), American Express (AXP), Bank of America (BAC), Coca-Cola (KO), and Chevron (CVX). Moody's (MCO), ranked from seventh to sixth. Occidental Petroleum (OXY), ranked from sixth to seventh. Chubb (CB), ranked Kraft Heinz (KHC), ranked Alphabet (GOOGL).

data.title: Web3 Trends in 2026: RWA Leads the Way, Four Key Areas to Watch
data.description:
In 2025, the Web3 industry underwent a transformation period of "de-bubbling and re-implementation," with cryptocurrency speculation gradually fading and real-world asset tokenization and technology-enabled real-world applications becoming the industry mainstream. In 2026, with improved compliance systems, deepened pilot scenarios, and breakthroughs in technological innovation, the Web3 industry will enter a period of "high-quality development," no longer pursuing "conceptual hype" but focusing on "practical value." Based on recent industry dynamics, policy guidance, and implementation cases, we predict that the Web3 industry will exhibit four core trends in 2026, with RWA leading the way, and digital RMB, cross-border interoperability, and technological integration developing simultaneously. Each direction holds immense opportunities and deserves close attention.

1. RWA Becomes the Core Engine of the Industry

By 2026, RWA will become the core growth engine of the Web3 industry, moving from "pilot exploration" to "large-scale implementation". There are three main reasons: First, a sound compliance system, with the implementation of the "RWA Asset Digitalization Compliance and Risk Control Standard", clarifying the boundaries of industry development and lowering the entry threshold for enterprises; second, policy support, with many regions in China including RWA in the key areas of digital economy development, and pilot programs in agriculture, cross-border e-commerce, and other fields continuing to advance; third, market demand, with the real economy needing new financing channels and investors needing investment targets that are "low-risk and backed by real assets," and RWA precisely meets the needs of both parties.

Predicting that in 2026, RWA will achieve three major breakthroughs: First, its application scenarios will be further broadened, extending from agriculture and cross-border e-commerce to more fields such as intellectual property, real estate, and supply chain finance; second, the participating entities will be diversified, with banks, insurance companies, and traditional asset management institutions all investing in RWA and launching related wealth management products and risk control services, in addition to technology companies; third, its scale will grow significantly, with the domestic RWA transaction volume expected to exceed 10 billion yuan, and the global market size moving towards the trillion-yuan level. 2. Deep Integration of Digital RMB and Web3 The arrival of the Digital RMB 2.0 era will promote its deep integration with Web3 technology, no longer limited to a "payment tool," but becoming a core carrier for "on-chain asset settlement and empowerment of real assets." In 2026, the integration of the two will present two major highlights: First, the digital yuan will become a core settlement tool for RWA, with more RWA projects adopting digital yuan for pricing and settlement, achieving seamless integration of "on-chain tokens and fiat currency," reducing cross-border settlement costs, and improving transaction efficiency. Second, the digital yuan will further penetrate various scenarios, leveraging Web3 technology to combine smart contracts with the digital yuan, and implementing applications in areas such as prepaid consumption, fiscal subsidies, and supply chain finance, solving pain points in traditional payments. For example, smart contracts can ensure that prepayments are not misappropriated, and on-chain traceability can ensure accurate distribution of fiscal subsidies. Simultaneously, the interest-bearing function and cross-border capabilities of the digital yuan will continue to be upgraded, and its integration with Web3 will further promote the internationalization of the yuan, creating a new model of "digital yuan + RWA + cross-border trade." 3. Cross-border connectivity becomes a key breakthrough. In 2026, cross-border connectivity in the Web3 industry will enter a "normalized phase." Hong Kong, as a global financial hub, will become a "core bridge" for mainland Web3 assets to connect with global capital, and the collaboration between the two places will continue to deepen. On the one hand, the connection between the Hong Kong RWA cross-border hub platform and the mainland's registered blockchain system will continue to be optimized, further improving the efficiency of mutual recognition of asset qualifications and data interoperability, and further reducing registration time and transaction cycles, allowing mainland assets (such as agricultural assets and new energy assets) to more easily connect with global investors. On the other hand, Hong Kong will launch more RWA cross-border pilot programs, expand the cross-border settlement scenarios of digital RMB, promote the implementation of the complete chain of "mainland assets on-chain—Hong Kong token issuance—global circulation," and consolidate its core position as an RWA cross-border hub. Furthermore, the multilateral central bank digital currency bridge (mBridge) project will continue to expand, covering more countries and regions. The proportion of digital RMB in cross-border settlement will further increase, promoting the popularization of Web3 technology in global cross-border trade. 4. AI+IoT+Blockchain Empowering Real Economy Upgrading By 2026, the Web3 industry will no longer be limited to a single blockchain technology, but will show a trend of "multi-technology integration." The deep integration of AI, IoT, and blockchain will further unleash the value of technology and empower the upgrading of the real economy. Specifically, AI + blockchain will be used for RWA asset risk control and valuation. By analyzing asset operation data through AI, accurate risk warnings and asset valuations can be achieved, improving the security and professionalism of RWA projects. IoT + blockchain will be used for monitoring physical assets, such as agricultural machinery in the agricultural sector and charging piles in the new energy sector. Operational data will be collected in real time through IoT devices, and the data will be tamper-proof after being uploaded to the blockchain, ensuring the authenticity and transparency of RWA assets. Cross-chain technology will continue to break through, breaking down the silos between different blockchain platforms, enabling the free flow of assets between different chains, and activating global liquidity.

-Conclusion-

If 2025 was the "Year of Transformation" for the Web3 industry, then 2026 will be the "Year of Value" for Web3—the industry will completely move away from "hype" and focus on "real-world empowerment," with RWA leading the way, and digital RMB, cross-border interoperability, and technological integration working in tandem to form a new development pattern of "compliance, implementation, and win-win."

For investors, it is necessary to abandon the "crypto speculation mindset" and focus on RWA projects with real asset backing and compliant implementation; for enterprises, it is necessary to seize policy opportunities, deploy in core areas such as RWA and digital RMB, and leverage Web3 technology to achieve digital transformation; for ordinary people, it is necessary to improve their understanding, see the technological trends clearly, and grasp the new opportunities brought by the digital economy.

In 2026, every breakthrough in the Web3 industry will drive the deep integration of the digital economy and the real economy. Let's look forward to the transformative changes brought about by this technological revolution.

data.title: The Scythe of Bitcoin's Leader: A True Account of a Nasdaq Robbery
data.description:
Author: Justin Bechler, Bitcoin OG; Translator: Ismay, BlockBeats

This article delves into the astonishing capital operations behind David Bailey and his controlled Nakamoto Holdings ($NAKA). From the frenzied surge during the backdoor listing to the 99% crash after retail investors entered the market, and then to the high-premium acquisition of his private assets by the disarrayed listed company, it is a meticulously designed wealth transfer that exploits information asymmetry and regulatory loopholes.

This is a brutal investigation into greed, compliance games, and influencer capitalism. It warns us that when faith is packaged as a financial product, and when the slogan of decentralization meets centralized greed, retail investors are often the last line of defense for liquidity exit. Understanding this story may help you be more clear-headed and less blindly follow the next big investor's recommendations.

The following is the full text: This morning, David Bailey used a publicly traded company whose market value had evaporated by 99% to acquire two private companies he founded at a fourfold premium to the current share price—all without any shareholder vote. What's most astonishing? This asset transfer was already planned long before retail investors bought the first share. To understand how this was done, we must start from the beginning. In May 2025, a zombie company called KindlyMD announced a merger with Nakamoto Holdings, the Bitcoin reserve founded by David Bailey. The share price soared from $2 to over $30 within days, as retail investors flocked in. Bitcoin enthusiasts celebrated wildly, with Bailey even comparing himself to the Morgan, Medici, and Rothschild families. Nine months later, the stock price plummeted to 29 cents, and Bailey had just used that stock to buy his own company. The Pump's mechanism was ingeniously designed. KindlyMD was originally an obscure micro-cap stock on Nasdaq. Nakamoto Holdings went public through a reverse merger, backed by $510 million in PIPE (private equity) funding and $200 million in convertible bonds. On paper, this appears to be the birth of a Bitcoin reserve giant, with a new generation of Bitcoin influencers clamoring to tell you why you should buy $NAKA (of course, the reason being to own more Bitcoin). Within days, NAKA's price-to-book ratio (Multiple-to-NAV) reached a staggering 23 times, meaning speculators paid $23 for every $1 of Bitcoin the company held. Michael Saylor's MicroStrategy never reached this premium. The difference lies in MicroStrategy's years of operating history, its revenue-generating software business, and a CEO who didn't manipulate transaction structures to line his own pockets on the back end. Insiders know secrets that retail investors don't. PIPE investors—including notorious BIP-110 opponents Udi Wertheimer, Jameson Lopp, and Adam Back—acquired their shares at $1.12 per share. Retail investors, on the other hand, bought in at $28, $30, $31, or even higher. This information asymmetry was ingrained in the structure from day one. In June, Bailey completed another $51.5 million PIPE funding round at $5.00 per share. The second batch of investors, although their entry cost was much lower than that of retail investors, was still far above the floor price of $1.12, and they were ultimately also fleeced. Bailey celebrated the completion of the financing, claiming it took less than 72 hours and that investor demand was extremely strong. Let's take a closer look at this strategy. The Dump By September, NAKA had plummeted by 96%. Early PIPE investors who acquired shares at $1.12 were finally able to cash out after the merger in August, and they did. Bailey's response was quite bizarre for a publicly traded company CEO; he told those shareholders who were just there to trade the swings to get out. So they really left. The stock price continued to fall. It fell below $1. It fell below 50 cents. It fell below 30 cents. A company holding approximately 5,765 bitcoins (worth over $500 million) now has a market capitalization of less than $300 million. The market's valuation of Nakamoto is even lower than the value of its bitcoins on its balance sheet, which speaks volumes about how investors view the management team and company structure wrapped around those bitcoins.

Debt Spiral

When the stock price crashed, Bailey frequently changed lenders, like a gambler borrowing money on the casino floor.

The initial capital structure included $200 million in convertible bonds from Yorkville Advisors, with a conversion price of $2.80. As NAKA's stock price fell below this price, the convertible bonds became debt that could potentially devour equity. Therefore, on October 3, Nakamoto borrowed a $203 million term loan from Two Prime Lending to redeem Yorkville's notes and interest. Four days later, on October 7th, they borrowed another $206 million in USDT from Antalpha at 7% interest to repay Two Prime. Antalpha's loan term was only 30 days (with an option to extend by 30 days). Within a week, they replaced the convertible bonds with term loans, and then replaced the term loans with 30-day bridge loans. The original plan was to convert this bridge loan into $250 million in 5-year secured convertible bonds from Antalpha. The new convertible bonds would then be used to repay the bridge loan, the bridge loan to repay the term loan, and the term loan to repay the old convertible bonds. However, that $250 million in convertible bonds never materialized according to Antalpha's terms. On December 16th, Nakamoto borrowed $210 million USDT from Kraken at an 8% interest rate, using Bitcoin from his national treasury as 150% overcollateral. Let's do the math: the lender had $315 million worth of Bitcoin as collateral for the $210 million loan. If Nakamoto's stock price went to zero, Kraken would take the collateral. If Bitcoin fell by 33%, Kraken would remain unscathed. At every stage of this drama, the lender was heavily protected, while common shareholders bore the brunt of the reflexive collapse. Every new loan is tightening the noose. The countdown has begun. On December 10th, Nasdaq notified Nakamoto that it faced delisting due to its stock price remaining below $1 for 30 consecutive trading days. The company must regain compliance by June 8th, 2026, meaning a closing price above $1 for 10 consecutive trading days. The current stock price is 29 cents. Once delisted, Nakamoto will be unable to conduct ATM (market price) offerings, issue convertible bonds, or use stock as acquisition currency. Everything Bailey assembled within this shell depends on a Nasdaq listing status that is currently unsustainable. Accounting Disaster In November, Nakamoto filed Form 12b-25 with the SEC, acknowledging that the accounting complexities resulting from the merger prevented it from filing quarterly financial statements on time. Preliminary data reveals the truth: The Nakamoto acquisition resulted in a $59.75 million loss (the payment price exceeded net asset value). Unrealized losses on digital assets amounted to $22.07 million. The sale of Bitcoin resulted in a $1.41 million realized loss. Refinancing rotations resulted in a $14.45 million debt repayment loss. The quarterly loss was approximately $97 million, only partially offset by a $21.8 million accounting gain on contingent liabilities. This company, which should have been the perfect Bitcoin reserve tool, couldn't even submit its ledgers on time. This brings us back to this morning. Nakamoto announced the signing of a definitive merger agreement to acquire BTC Inc and UTXO Management. BTC Inc owns Bitcoin Magazine and runs Bitcoin conferences. UTXO manages a Bitcoin-focused hedge fund. Bailey is the chairman and CEO of the buyer, Nakamoto. He is also the founder of the sellers, BTC Inc and UTXO. He is the buyer, the seller, and the CEO who approved the terms. However, just weeks before the acquisition, he quietly handed over the CEO title to Brandon Greene, creating an extremely thin barrier between himself and the entity he was about to acquire with shareholder equity. This morning's trading was entirely financed through Nakamoto stock, priced at $1.12 based on call options embedded in the original marketing service agreement. $NAKA is currently struggling to climb back to $0.29. Bailey's company received shares valued at almost four times the current market price. Holders of BTC Inc and UTXO securities will receive 363.6 million shares, a deal worth $107.3 million at market price. However, these shares were issued at $1.12, meaning the trade was constructed when NAKA's stock price soared, and the terms were never adjusted when the price crashed. Forget the fictitious pricing in the contract. What really matters is that 363.6 million new shares have just entered the circulating supply. Whether the document states $1.12 or $0.29, existing shareholders are diluted by this amount. The $1.12 label is a concession to the seller, but the dilution is real. No additional shareholder approval is needed because call options were already embedded in the initial merger documents, which shareholders voted to approve when NAKA's stock price was still around $20 or $30. The retail investors who approved these terms had no idea that they were authorizing a future private acquisition of Bailey's business at a locked-in premium, while their shares were vanishing. A self-serving transaction structure. Looking back, the entire structure is breathtakingly elegant. Bailey created Nakamoto Holdings. He merged it into a publicly traded shell company through KindlyMD, raising $710 million in the process. Fueled by retail investor enthusiasm, the stock price was driven up to 23 times NAV. PIPE investors entered at $1.12, while the public paid 20 to 30 times that figure. The stock price subsequently plummeted 99%. During this period, the company changed lenders three times in a week, attempting to manage $200 million in debt that was originally structured to be converted into equity when the stock price was significantly higher than it was then. Now, with the stock price plummeting below 30 cents, Bailey is using this hollowed-out tool to swallow up his private empire, according to terms agreed upon when the stock was still a hundred times its initial value. The initial KindlyMD merger was a Trojan horse; the acquisition of BTC Inc. is the real payload. Bailey told us this from the beginning. In the initial press release, he said Nakamoto would acquire BTC Inc., subject to audits and the exercise of call options. The MSA was publicly filed, and the option terms were disclosed. Everything was legally compliant and completely transparent—like all complex financial engineering, the truth was buried in piles of documents no one would read. This man, who runs Bitcoin Magazine, organizes the world's largest Bitcoin conference, and positions himself as a leader of the Bitcoin movement, built a publicly traded company, destroyed 99% of shareholder value, and is now using it to acquire his own company at a premium. He once compared himself to the Medici family. At least the Medici family created value for Florence before taking a cut. Nakamoto is the monstrosity that arises when influencer culture meets the public stock market. Exiting Liquidity David Bailey raised $710 million from over 200 investors across six continents. He promised them a future like the Morgans, the Medici, the Rothschilds—a financial dynasty built on Bitcoin. He told them Nakamoto would bring Bitcoin to the center of global capital markets. He said their names would resonate in history. But he delivered 99% losses. He priced PIPE at $1.12, while retail investors bought it at $28. He inserted a call option to acquire his own company into the filings without the shareholders understanding the mandate. He switched lenders three times in a week to prevent $200 million in debt from crushing the equity, accumulating $14 million in debt repayment losses in the process. He sold Bitcoin at a loss from the national treasury, which was supposed to be held only. He couldn't even file quarterly financial reports on time. And when the stock price finally plummeted to 29 cents... When the rubble was cleared and the retail investors who trusted him were wiped out, he exercised that call option, using the remnants of their investments to buy his own private empire at four times the market price. Bailey held 11 million shares at a cost of $1.12. Adam Back held nearly 9 million shares. Balaji, Lopp, Yusko, Salinas, and Wu Jihan—their entry prices were prices that teachers, truck drivers, or first-time investors could never afford. These are the people who shape the Bitcoin narrative. They run conferences, publish magazines, manage funds, and tweet. They are the supply chain of the faith, turning skeptics into believers and believers into suckers. Now, Bailey owns Bitcoin Magazine, Bitcoin conferences, and a hedge fund, all crammed into a publicly traded company with a market capitalization only a fraction of its Bitcoin holdings. All acquisitions were made with four times the market value of the shares, all approved before retail investors even had a penny to their name. And he's not done yet. Nakamoto has filed for a $5 billion ATM (market value) stock offering with the SEC. Bailey now controls the media division, the conference division, the hedge fund, and a shelf registration that allows him to continue issuing shares backed by the Bitcoin Treasury until the last drop of value is squeezed out. When exactly did the Bitcoin community hand over the keys to conference promoters and influencer capitalists? And why are people surprised when they drive off?
